description: Using the wrong operand width or assuming a sub-register write clears the full register, corrupting adjacent bits or memory.
body: Failure mode: code writes an 8- or 16-bit sub-register expecting the upper bits to be cleared (they are not on x86-64, unlike a 32-bit write which zero-extends), or uses the wrong load/store width, so stale high bits leak into a later use or the wrong number of bytes are touched in memory. Detection signals: a value has unexpected high bits set; a memory write corrupts an adjacent field; the result is correct for small inputs but wrong once high bits matter. Do not assume sub-register writes clear the rest. Safer fallback: use the correct operand width for the data type, explicitly zero- or sign-extend when needed, match load/store widths to the field size, and verify the loaded/stored bytes by stepping in the debugger against a reference.
